questions.question: Define AVL tree and explain the balance factor condition that must be satisfied by all nodes.
keyPoints: AVL tree is a self-balancing binary search tree
Balance factor = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree)
Balance factor must be -1, 0, or +1 for every node
Named after Adelson-Velsky and Landis who invented it in 1962

questions.question: Explain the four rotation cases in AVL trees with appropriate examples.
keyPoints: LL Case: Left-left imbalance requires single right rotation
RR Case: Right-right imbalance requires single left rotation
LR Case: Left-right imbalance requires double rotation (left then right)
RL Case: Right-left imbalance requires double rotation (right then left)

questions.question: State and explain the five properties of Red-Black trees.
keyPoints: Every node is either red or black
Root is always black
All leaves (NIL) are black
Red node cannot have red children (no two consecutive red nodes)
All paths from any node to descendant leaves have same black height

questions.question: What is the time complexity of search, insert, and delete operations in AVL trees? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: All operations take O(log n) time in worst case
Height of AVL tree is bounded by approximately 1.44*log2(n)
This is proven using recurrence relation for minimum nodes
Balanced nature guarantees logarithmic height

questions.question: Explain why B-trees are preferred over binary search trees for database indexing.
keyPoints: B-trees have high branching factor reducing tree height
Fewer disk I/O operations needed (height = logm n)
All leaves at same level ensuring balanced access time
Suitable for systems with large data stored on disk

questions.question: Differentiate between 2-3 trees and B-trees with respect to their structure and applications.
keyPoints: 2-3 trees are a special case of B-trees with order 3
2-3 trees can have 2 or 3 children per node
B-trees have order m where m can be large
B-trees optimized for disk access, 2-3 trees for memory

questions.question: What is double rotation in AVL trees? When is it required? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Double rotation needed for LR and RL imbalance cases
First rotate the child in opposite direction to the main imbalance
Then rotate the unbalanced node in the direction of imbalance
Example: inserting 10 in tree with root 20, left child 15 shows LR case

questions.question: Insert the following keys sequentially into an AVL tree: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 25. Show the tree after each insertion and identify when rotations are performed.
keyPoints: Insert 10: single node tree
Insert 20: becomes right child of 10
Insert 30: causes RR imbalance, performs left rotation
Insert 40: goes to right of 30
Insert 50: causes RR imbalance, performs left rotation
Insert 25: causes LR imbalance, requires double rotation

questions.question: Compare AVL trees and Red-Black trees with respect to balancing criteria, height, rotations required, and suitable applications.
keyPoints: AVL: balance factor ≤1, RB: black height equal on all paths
AVL height ≤ 1.44*log(n), RB height ≤ 2*log(n+1)
AVL requires more rotations, RB requires fewer but may recolor more
AVL better for read-heavy, RB better for frequent insertions

questions.question: Explain the algorithm for insertion in a Red-Black tree. Discuss the cases that require rotations and how the tree maintains its properties.
keyPoints: Insert new node as red leaf (standard BST insertion)
If parent is black, done. If parent is red, handle cases:
Case 1: Uncle is red - recolor parent, uncle, grandparent
Case 2: Uncle is black, triangle - single rotation
Case 3: Uncle is black, line - rotate and recolor
Continue fixing violations up the tree until root

questions.question: Define a B-tree of order m. Explain the properties and derive the minimum and maximum number of keys in a B-tree of height h.
keyPoints: B-tree of order m: each node has at most m children
Minimum children for non-root: ceil(m/2)
Maximum children: m
Minimum keys: ceil(m/2) - 1 for non-root
Maximum keys: m-1
Height h tree: minimum keys = 2*t^(h-1) - 1, maximum = m^h - 1
